metrics.label: Cadence
metrics.note: Most runners are efficient at 170-185 steps/min. Low cadence usually means overstriding.

metrics.label: Pelvic drop
metrics.note: The hip of the swinging leg dropping >~10 deg points to weak hip stabilizers (injury risk).

metrics.label: Pronation (estimate)
metrics.note: Estimated rear-foot roll-in at contact. This is a low-confidence 2-D rear-view estimate — treat it as a flag to check your shoe wear and ankle, not a measurement.

metrics.label: Step width / crossover
metrics.note: Feet should not cross the midline. Crossover narrows your base and stresses the IT band.

metrics.label: Lateral trunk sway
metrics.note: Side-to-side lean of the upper body. A lot of sway often follows hip drop or a weak core.

metrics.label: Trunk Pelvis Rotation
metrics.note: Shoulder-vs-pelvis counter-rotation — a low-confidence 2-D rear-view proxy.

metrics.label: Head lateral sway
metrics.note: Side-to-side head movement per stride (rear view). Excess sway signals poor upper-body stability.

feedback.title: Left/right imbalance: Pronation (estimate)
feedback.detail: Pronation (estimate) differs 73% between sides (L 6 vs R 12 deg), with the right side standing out. Imbalances over ~10% are worth addressing before they cause one-sided overuse.
feedback.cue: Give the right side a little extra attention in strength work.
feedback.drill: Single-leg strength on the weaker side; film again in ~4 weeks to recheck.

feedback.title: Left/right imbalance: Pelvic drop
feedback.detail: Pelvic drop differs 36% between sides (L 5 vs R 8 deg), with the right side standing out. Imbalances over ~10% are worth addressing before they cause one-sided overuse.
feedback.cue: Give the right side a little extra attention in strength work.
feedback.drill: Single-leg strength on the weaker side; film again in ~4 weeks to recheck.

feedback.title: Hip drop (weak stabilizers)
feedback.detail: Your pelvis drops about 8 deg toward the swinging leg. Excess pelvic drop points to weak hip stabilizers and is linked to IT-band, knee, and hip pain.
feedback.cue: Run 'level hips' — imagine balancing a cup of water on each hip.
feedback.drill: Hip strength: single-leg squats, side planks, banded hip-hikes, 3×/week.

exercises.name: Single-leg strength (weaker side)
exercises.why: Closes a left/right gap by training the lagging side.
exercises.dose: Extra 1-2 sets on the weaker side (squats, calf raises, hip-hikes)
exercises.progression: Re-test in ~4 weeks; even up once matched.

exercises.name: Side planks
exercises.why: Builds lateral hip/core endurance to keep the pelvis level.

exercises.name: Banded hip-hikes
exercises.why: Directly trains the hip abductors that stop the drop.
exercises.progression: Add load / standing on a step.

exercises.name: Single-leg squats
exercises.why: Controls hip + knee under bodyweight on one leg.

quality.message: At 60 fps, ground-contact timing is approximate — use 120/240 fps slow-mo for it.
